Seam contradiction (KEEP main + new contract) closed via explicit Phase-1 main-handler regeneration with its own Opus author + R1+R2 pair. The grep-proof exit gate ('main's IPC handlers all reference generated contract types') is verifiable.

Reachability assertion regression fixed properly: hand-pinned `EXPECTED` constant replaced with `__all__`-vs-mounted set comparison at startup, sourced from `gateway/routers/__init__.py`. Adding a router without registering it now fails the next startup.

Approval #0 preconditions enumerate every missing dependency the prior critique caught (nx, protoc, ts-proto, betterproto, qwen2.5-coder:32b, Groq/OpenRouter/OpenAI keys, gateway/ tree, orchestrator/ tree, LiteLLM) with realistic time costs and explicit framing as outside the autonomous wall-clock.
Worktree pool sized 12 (not 50) with disk + CPU envelope math shown; '500-agent dispatchable' clarified as 500 total claims processed serially, not 500 concurrent.
Approval-prompt accounting is honest: 5 batched + 2-7 runtime = ~11 total realistic. The previous '<5' framing called out as misleading.
LiteLLM concretely named with version pin (`pip install litellm`) and orchestrator hook (`orchestrator/router.py`) — brain-agnostic premise is now implementable, not hand-waved.
Token target raised from <500k to <2.5M target / 4.0M hard cap with explicit arithmetic showing 500-800k goes to R1+R2 alone. The prior budget left no room for workers.
Drain ceiling raised from 2 min to 5 min with explicit `abandoned` status for non-cancelable remote inferences — honest about what's actually cancelable.

Anthropic weekly cap reframed as heuristic (last-7-days ledger + 429 timestamps) checked at kickoff and Phase-3 exit only, not every boundary. Prevents the heuristic from nuking an otherwise-finishing run.
famtastic-dna.md cross-reference removed; desktop-renderer-rules.md authored in Phase 1 instead. Critique was right those site-factory rules don't apply.
Free-tier rpm math corrected to per-key from per-caller. ~80 rpm aggregate ceiling, not 100.

Kanban / Sessions-write / Skills / Messaging / Hooks now their own packages or named sub-features with author roles explicitly routed to Opus rather than free-tier. Acknowledges these are not boilerplate.
Response-to-critique section names what was conceded and what was not — verdict, brain-agnostic premise, SQLite ledger, Vite glob, failure budget — each defended on merits.

major_issues.claim: Plan claims bundling Gemma 3 4B / Qwen 2.5 3B inside the IPA/APK is 'App Store guideline-safe; no post-install model download.'
major_issues.rebuttal: Bundling does not sidestep review. Apple Guideline 2.5.2 prohibits apps that download, install, or execute code which changes features or functionality, and Apple reviewers have increasingly flagged on-device LLMs that can produce arbitrary executable text (Vibecode/Replit-style takedowns happened even with bundled stacks). More concretely: a ~1.6–1.8GB bundled model violates Guideline 4.2 (minimum functionality) only marginally, but it hits Guideline 2.4.1 (binary size review scrutiny) and 5.1.1 (data collection) hard. Google Play's Generative AI policy (2024 update, enforced through 2026) requires output testing documentation and red-team evidence for any on-device GenAI — bundling does not exempt this; it intensifies it because the model ships pre-installed. Risk register #1 understates: Apple specifically rejects apps using on-device LLMs that lack content filtering at the model layer, and a 3–4B Q4 model has trivially defeated safety filters in 2025 jailbreak research. Likely rejection under 1.1 (objectionable content) and 1.2 (user-generated content) absent demonstrable on-device moderation.

major_issues.claim: Phase 4 'Computer-use bridge' (phone-as-thin-client to home Mac, touch→mouse, screencast over Tailscale) 'cleanly stays inside App Store Guideline 2.5.2.'
major_issues.rebuttal: This is the highest-risk feature in the plan and the claim it stays inside 2.5.2 is wrong on its face. Apple has repeatedly rejected or pulled remote-desktop-style apps that allow arbitrary code execution on a paired machine driven from iOS (see longstanding restrictions on apps like UTM SE, iSH, and the historical Steam Link friction). Even when execution is technically on the remote machine, Guideline 2.5.2 covers apps whose 'primary purpose' is to provide a way to execute arbitrary code. A general-purpose 'drive any UI on your Mac, dispatch keystrokes and clicks' bridge will be evaluated as remote code execution. Additionally, 4.2.7 (remote desktop clients) imposes strict requirements: must be business/enterprise use, host must be owned by the user, no general-purpose code execution, no third-party app store surface, and B2C remote desktop has been disallowed in multiple review cycles. Google Play has parallel restrictions under the Device and Network Abuse policy when remote control extends to executing shell commands. The 'phone never executes — it just paints and dispatches' framing does not save you; reviewers look at end-to-end behavior. Likely rejection; needs to be gated behind a separate enterprise distribution channel or removed.

major_issues.claim: Voice features ship with 'Background voice (locked phone)' as a default capability, and Background Modes include 'audio' for voice continuation.
major_issues.rebuttal: Apple Guideline 2.5.4 restricts `UIBackgroundModes=audio` to apps that 'provide audible content to the user while in the background' — meaning actively producing or recording audio the user has explicitly initiated. Using it as a general 'keep the AI voice session alive while locked' channel has been a common rejection reason; Apple has specifically rejected meditation/coaching apps that abuse audio background mode for non-playback continuation. The voiceover/Realtime use case is borderline: Apple permits it during an active voice call-like session (CallKit-backed), but a passive AI voice that continues in the background without CallKit will trigger rejection under 2.5.4. On Android, FGS with `mediaPlayback` requires actual media playback per Android 14 policy; using it for STT/agent voice is a Play policy violation (Foreground Services policy, updated 2024). Plan should either integrate CallKit (iOS) and use `microphone` FGS type with the 'phone'/`voice` justification (Android 14+), or drop background voice from defaults.

major_issues.claim: Per-message brain switcher and 'BYOK option' make routing user-supplied keys to Anthropic/OpenAI/Google fine for App Store.
major_issues.rebuttal: Apple Guideline 3.1.1 (in-app purchase) and 3.1.3(b) (multiplatform services / reader rule) interact poorly here. If Shay's subscription includes any paid relay/cost beyond user-supplied keys, Apple requires IAP for digital content/services consumed in the app. The 'cost/latency badge' showing per-message cost implies pricing surfaced in app, which triggers 3.1.1 scrutiny on whether Apple gets its 30%/15% on cloud token spend that flows through. BYOK is acceptable in principle (multiple AI apps do it), but the plan must (a) not surface token-pack purchases in-app outside IAP, and (b) handle the External Link Account Entitlement (3.1.3(a)) properly if any account creation flow links to a website billing page. Additionally Guideline 5.1.2(i) is cited but the plan does not address 5.1.1(i) — data collection consent screens are required PER PROVIDER, not aggregated. Likely revisions needed at submission.

major_issues.claim: App Intents catalog with `ApproveNextToolCall`, `SwitchBrain`, `StartAgentRun` is 'free distribution channel' via Apple Intelligence.
major_issues.rebuttal: Several of these intents trigger destructive or security-sensitive operations (approving tool calls that may write files, push code, exec shell on the desk). Apple's App Intents guidance and the Siri/Shortcuts review path require that destructive intents be marked as such and require user confirmation; voice-driven 'approve next tool call' chained through Apple Intelligence will be evaluated under Guideline 2.5.1 (correct API use) and 5.1.1 (privacy/auth). 'StartAgentRun(brief, brain)' triggered by a third-party voice composition without re-auth is exactly the pattern Apple flags. Likely required: every destructive intent must require biometric re-auth before execution, which breaks the 'compose with other apps' positioning.

major_issues.claim: Live Activities with APNs `frequent-updates` entitlement and 8-hour windows are presented as a default-on capability.
major_issues.rebuttal: The `NSSupportsLiveActivitiesFrequentUpdates` key does not grant unlimited budget; Apple throttles based on user behavior and system thermal state. The 8-hour window for ActivityKit is documented as best-effort and is regularly truncated to 4 hours under iOS power constraints. More importantly: APNs `apns-priority: 10` with `apns-push-type: liveactivity` is rate-limited at the server and at the device, and Apple has explicitly warned that abusing frequent-updates leads to silent throttling app-wide. Plan should not claim 'AgentRun activity, 8hr window' as a default — it should be presented as 'up to 8h, throttled by iOS.' Not a rejection risk per se, but a UX/correctness risk worth flagging at major severity because the entire 'agent runs you can watch from the Lock Screen' narrative hinges on it.

minor_issues: Google Play Generative AI policy requires an in-app user reporting mechanism for objectionable AI output AND documented red-team evidence at submission — plan mentions the report button but not the red-team documentation requirement, which has caused first-submission delays of 2–4 weeks in 2025/2026.
Apple Sign-In is required (Guideline 4.8) if Google Sign-In is offered — plan offers both but does not explicitly note the parity requirement.

Watch app with 'Approve/Cancel' for destructive actions needs to handle the case where the watch is unlocked but the phone is locked — Apple has rejected apps that allow consequential actions from watch without phone-side biometric.

CarPlay 'chat + voice only' still requires CarPlay entitlement which Apple grants sparingly and only for navigation, audio, communication, EV charging, parking, quick food ordering, and driving task categories. 'AI chat in the car' does not fit any current CarPlay category — likely entitlement denial.
HealthKit read access requires a documented medical/fitness use case in App Review notes; 'routed prompts' is not a sufficient justification and will be rejected under Guideline 5.1.3.
iOS 26 / Foundation Models availability claim assumes API exposure that as of submission may still be limited; should hedge.
Share Extension memory budget cited as ~120MB is closer to 60MB in practice on non-Plus devices — even tighter constraint than plan acknowledges.

Bundled model size + App Thinning / Asset Delivery: Play Asset Delivery has a 1GB cap per install-time asset pack and total APK size limits; a 1.5GB Qwen bundle will require on-demand delivery, contradicting the 'no post-install download' claim made for Apple.

Continuity Handoff requires both apps share the same Team ID and registered activity types — the Mac Studio is described as a Node service, not a registered macOS app, so handoff will not work as described without a real macOS app target.

Privacy Nutrition Label per-provider disclosure is correct in principle but the plan does not address that Anthropic/OpenAI/Google each have different data-retention defaults that must be disclosed separately — this is a common rejection cause.
strengths: Correctly identifies that keeping execution on the desk runtime is the right architectural posture for App Store risk minimization (even if it does not fully solve the problem).
Explicit Guideline 2.5.2 framing for bundled models (no post-install download) is the correct instinct, though incomplete.
Recognizes that Realtime / background voice and Live Activities are the high-leverage but high-policy-risk surfaces.
Acknowledges Android 14 foreground service restrictions even if the proposed mitigation is thin.
Privacy Nutrition Label / Data Safety form parity is called out, which is often forgotten in plans of this scope.
Correctly identifies that Apple Foundation Models is Swift-only as a public framework API.
Correctly notes Gemini Nano's foreground-only restriction on Android as a real ML Kit GenAI constraint.
Correctly identifies the 4K context ceiling on Apple Foundation Models and routes long-context work elsewhere.
Correctly distinguishes Image Description (ML Kit GenAI surface) from raw Nano access, and acknowledges device-tier gating.
Honest about needing a cloud fallback when on-device output is poor.

major_issues.claim: Bundled Gemma 3 4B / Qwen 2.5 3B are 'shipped inside the IPA/APK at install time' as 'Apple App Store guideline-safe; no post-install model download.'
major_issues.rebuttal: The plan later contradicts itself by recommending App Thinning / Asset Delivery to 'stream the bundled model only to devices that need it' — that IS post-install download via On-Demand Resources or Play Asset Delivery, not bundling. The two strategies are opposite. On Android, Play policy actually requires Asset Delivery for large models because the base APK has a 150MB ceiling; a 1.5GB bundled model will be rejected. On iOS, a 1.6–1.8GB always-bundled model hurts install conversion and review. The compliance narrative is internally inconsistent and incorrect.

major_issues.claim: Apple Foundation Models on iOS 26+ is the universal on-device routing layer where 'every prompt is classified locally before routing.'
major_issues.rebuttal: Apple FM is gated to Apple Intelligence-eligible devices (iPhone 15 Pro / 16 series and newer, M-series iPads/Macs). For an indie shipping in 2026 this is a minority of active iPhones. The draft treats it as ubiquitous when it applies to roughly 30–40% of the active iOS install base, with the rest covered by a bundled-Gemma fallback that is itself problematic (see above). Realistic posture: FM is a nice-to-have on eligible devices, not a foundation you can build the routing layer on.

major_issues.claim: Gemini Nano is used as a general-purpose on-device LLM for arbitrary classification, summarization, and Image Description on Pixel 9+/S24+/OnePlus 13/Razr 60 Ultra/HONOR Magic 7.
major_issues.rebuttal: Gemini Nano is NOT a general-purpose third-party SDK. Public access is via ML Kit GenAI's narrow task APIs (Summarization, Proofreading, Rewriting, Image Description) — not free-form prompting. Third-party apps cannot directly invoke AICore for arbitrary classification prompts. The 'route every prompt through Nano for intent classification' design is not buildable with current public APIs. Stable third-party ML Kit GenAI access is also narrower than the device list claims — Pixel 9 / Galaxy S24+ tier is solid; the rest are partial.

major_issues.claim: On-device LLM triage layer where 'every prompt is classified locally before routing; routine summarization and structured-output extraction never leave the phone.'
major_issues.rebuttal: This is the load-bearing claim of the architecture and it is aspirational. On iOS only AI-eligible devices get FM; on Android only ML Kit GenAI's narrow task APIs are public and 'classification' is not one of them. What CAN be done today is non-LLM on-device classification (Apple NLTagger / NLClassifier, ML Kit text classification, custom CoreML). The plan should distinguish 'on-device ML classification' (real, ubiquitous) from 'on-device LLM prompting' (aspirational, fragmented, device-gated). Conflating them inflates what V1 of the app can actually deliver.

major_issues.claim: Apple Foundation Models multimodal is 'not exposed in FM as public API; route to GPT-4o or Gemini' — implying it exists but is restricted.
major_issues.rebuttal: Apple FM's on-device model is text-only; there is no hidden multimodal capability being withheld. The phrasing suggests an artificial restriction when it is a model-capability limit. This matters because the plan implies on-device captioning of shared images on iOS — that path requires Apple Vision (OCR/classification only, not generative captioning) or a third-party multimodal model, neither of which is FM.

major_issues.claim: WhisperKit Large V3 Turbo bundled as a viable default option alongside Gemma 3 4B, totaling roughly 3.4GB of bundled models before app code.
major_issues.rebuttal: Apple SpeechAnalyzer (iOS 26+) is now the recommended on-device STT and ships with the OS, so it should be the default with WhisperKit reserved as a fallback for older iOS. Treating WhisperKit Large as a bundled option compounds the size/compliance problems above and is not the right primary path.

minor_issues: Apple Intelligence's 'spring-2026 third-party voice composition' chaining App Intents is plausible roadmap but not confirmed shipping; treat as dependency, not given.
'KMP grew 23% in 18 months (JavaCodeGeeks survey)' — citation is thin and not load-bearing; source properly or drop.
Matrix says Claude voice is 'pipelined' (not realtime); Claude shipped a realtime-style voice mode in 2025, so the row may be stale.
Wear OS tile '20s refresh floor' framing makes tiles sound more limited than they are — event-driven updates are allowed.
The 'cost/latency badge from rolling p50' is good, but on-device intent classification before every send adds its own latency the draft doesn't budget against the 'feels instant' promise.

Routing decision tree omits the case where FM/Nano are available but user explicitly picked a cloud brain; prose claim 'every prompt is classified locally before routing' is heavier than the tree shows.

strengths: Brain-agnostic router with per-message switching is a genuinely differentiated product wedge nobody is currently shipping — the strategic positioning is sound.
The phone-as-control-surface / desk-as-runtime split is the correct architectural insight and cleanly sidesteps App Store Guideline 2.5.2.
On-device routing tree (FM/Nano for classification, cloud for reasoning) is a sensible, well-reasoned split that respects FM's 4K ceiling.
Risk register includes the actually-correct risks (2.5.2 rejection, FM context limit, Tailscale onboarding friction, Share Extension memory cap) rather than imaginary ones.
Phased TestFlight exit gates are well-defined and each phase is independently shippable — good de-risking structure for a solo build.

major_issues.claim: 14 weeks Fritz-solo timeline (360 Fritz-hours + 250 AI-paired hours) is fantasy. The repo shows ~61 Swift files in shay-desktop (macOS) but ZERO Kotlin files anywhere in famtastic. Fritz has no demonstrated Android/Kotlin shipping history, no demonstrated KMP experience, no demonstrated Live Activities experience, no demonstrated watchOS experience, no demonstrated Wear OS experience, no demonstrated WebRTC experience, no demonstrated APNs frequent-updates experience, no demonstrated MCP-client-implementation experience, no demonstrated WhisperKit/MLC-LLM bundled-model experience. Each of those is a 2-6 week learning curve on its own for a competent dev; Fritz is being asked to absorb 8-10 of them in parallel. Realistic solo-dev estimate: 9-15 months to Phase 4, not 14 weeks. The plan needs to either (a) cut scope to ~Phase 1-2 only, (b) explicitly budget for hiring a contractor for Android + watchOS + Live Activities, or (c) admit the 14-week number is aspirational and triple it.
major_issues.rebuttal: The hour budget assumes parity-level execution velocity in technologies Fritz has never shipped. Live Activities alone — APNs push-to-start, ActivityKit, frequent-updates entitlement approval from Apple, 4KB payload optimization, Dynamic Island layouts — is a 3-4 week ramp for a solo dev who hasn't done it. Watch + Wear together is another 4-6 weeks. WebRTC thin-client (Phase 4) is a 6-8 week project on its own at production quality. The 'AI-paired' column quietly assumes Codex/Claude can write production-quality Swift LiveActivity / watchOS / KMP code with negligible Fritz oversight, which is not how AI pairing works in domains the human can't review.

major_issues.claim: Kotlin Multiplatform pick is wrong for Fritz specifically. The defense correctly identifies that the hard surfaces are native-only (FM, Live Activities, App Intents, Watch, Wear), but then picks a stack that requires Fritz to ALSO become productive in Kotlin + Compose + KMP toolchain (Gradle, Konan, expect/actual, cocoapods-plugin) on top of native Swift. The repo evidence shows Fritz writes Swift (61 files in shay-desktop) but has shipped zero Kotlin. KMP's whole value prop is shared business logic — but Fritz is one person who will not realistically maintain two front-ends. A more honest pick is: ship iOS-only for V1 (Phase 1-3), defer Android entirely to Phase 5+. iOS-only captures Fritz's actual user (himself) on his actual device, lets him use SwiftUI/SwiftData/ActivityKit/WatchKit natively without a KMP abstraction tax, and removes ~40% of the build surface (Compose UI, Wear tile, Google Sign-In, Play Store generative-AI policy review, FGS lifecycle, ML Kit GenAI integration, MediaPipe LLM). The plan should defend why Android Day 1 is worth doubling the solo dev's workload when Fritz himself uses an iPhone.
major_issues.rebuttal: KMP requires Gradle expertise, expect/actual discipline, and a separate Compose UI codebase. The shared core the plan describes (brain router, MCP client, conversation graph, encryption, offline queue, sync) is ~30% of the code; the platform-specific UI + native bridges are ~70%. The shared-core savings do not justify the dual-platform tax for a solo dev whose primary user is himself on iOS.

major_issues.claim: Phase 4 'phone-as-thin-client for the desk Studio' (WebRTC screencast + touch→mouse + soft-keyboard→keystroke over Tailscale) is a separate product, not a feature. Building a production-grade remote-desktop client is a 6-12 month project for a specialist team (see Parsec, Moonlight, Jump Desktop). Latency tuning, codec selection (H.265 vs AV1), TURN fallback when Tailscale degrades, gesture-to-input translation, soft-keyboard IME handling, and App Store review of a remote-control app (Apple has historically scrutinized these heavily under 4.2 'minimum functionality' and 2.5.2) are each multi-week problems. Allocating ~4 weeks of one phase to this is not credible. Recommend deleting Phase 4 entirely or splitting it into its own V2 roadmap.
major_issues.rebuttal: Existing thin-client products (Jump, Screens, Parsec) have teams of 5-20 engineers and still ship with rough edges. A solo dev cannot ship a production WebRTC thin-client in 100 hours.

major_issues.claim: Bundled on-device models (Gemma 3 4B / Qwen 2.5 3B at 1.5-1.8GB) inside the IPA/APK violates the realistic shipping model. Bundling 1.8GB means every install — including TestFlight, including OTA deltas — ships that weight, and Apple discourages it. The plan waves at 'App Thinning' and 'Asset Delivery' but App Thinning does not strip variant-shared resources, and Play Asset Delivery requires the model be authored as an asset pack with specific install-time/on-demand semantics. More importantly, the plan claims Apple Guideline 2.5.2 forbids downloading model weights post-install — but model weights are data, not executable code, and EVERY shipping AI app (Apple Intelligence itself, Gemini Nano via Play Services, MLC Chat, Private LLM, PocketPal) downloads them post-install. Fritz should download the offline model post-install via a normal asset CDN, gated behind user opt-in. This is the standard pattern and is explicitly allowed.
major_issues.rebuttal: The plan's claim that bundling-vs-downloading is a 2.5.2 question is wrong. Apple's own Foundation Models are downloaded, not bundled. Bundling a 1.8GB model needlessly inflates IPA size for the majority of users who will never use offline mode.

major_issues.claim: MCP client on the phone is a real feature but the plan understates its cost. MCP is a stdio/SSE protocol designed for local processes. Speaking MCP from a phone over Tailscale to a desk-hosted MCP server requires either (a) a custom HTTP+SSE bridge written on both ends, or (b) routing every MCP call through shay-relay as an opaque proxy. Tool schemas, sampling, resources, prompts, and roots all need to be marshaled across the WAN. Schema drift between client and server SDK versions is a known MCP pain point. Building this as part of KMP shared core (so it works identically on iOS and Android) adds another expect/actual surface. Plan should either spec the wire format explicitly or scope MCP as 'phone displays the desk's tool registry, calls are proxied through shay-relay opaquely' — much smaller and probably what was meant.
major_issues.rebuttal: Implementing a real MCP client in pure Kotlin (so it compiles to both iOS and Android via KMP) means re-implementing the protocol from scratch — Anthropic's official SDKs are Python and TypeScript only. That is a multi-week engineering effort and an ongoing maintenance burden as the spec evolves.

major_issues.claim: The capability matrix conflates 'capability Shay COULD ship' with 'capability Shay WILL ship in 14 weeks.' Many checks in the Shay target column (CarPlay, HealthKit, Continuity Handoff, Wear OS tile with approval, Watch app with full per-run drill-down, basic-memory bidirectional read, Smart Connections WebGL graph, screen share during voice) are each 2-4 week features individually. The 14-row 'beats every incumbent' claim is the load-bearing strategic argument of the doc, but it relies on shipping all 14 simultaneously. Recommend marking each check with the phase it appears in, and being honest that V1 (Phase 1+2) only beats incumbents on ~5 rows: per-message brain switcher, brain-independent voice channel, MCP client, on-device LLM, cross-brain context handoff. That's still a real wedge — but it's a different positioning story.
major_issues.rebuttal: The capability matrix is the strategic anchor of the plan, and if 9 of the 14 winning rows are deferred to Phase 3 and Phase 4 (which may never ship realistically), the V1 differentiation story is much smaller than advertised.

minor_issues: Tailscale dependency is acknowledged but the 'guided onboarding with embedded SSO' mitigation is hand-wavy — Tailscale's iOS SDK does not support embedded sign-in in the way described; user has to install the Tailscale app separately and consent to the VPN profile. This is a real onboarding cliff.
Apple's 'frequent-updates' entitlement for Live Activities is not auto-granted; it requires justification in App Review and is sometimes denied. Plan should note review risk.
Critical Alerts entitlement requires separate Apple approval and is rarely granted to non-emergency apps. Listing it as 'user toggleable' implies it will be available — it probably won't be.
Cost estimate ignores Tailscale onboarding engineering, certificate rotation for APNs auth keys, and the time-cost of Apple Developer Program reviews for each new entitlement.

'Apple Intelligence spring-2026 third-party voice composition' is treated as a confirmed distribution channel; its actual surface area for third-party App Intents remains constrained and should not be load-bearing.

Brain provider TOS risk is rated 'Low' but OpenAI and Anthropic both have terms that constrain how their models can be presented alongside competitors; the in-product 'cost/latency badge' UX directly comparison-shops them. At minimum 'Medium' and worth a real legal pass.

Voice channel 'Gemini Live default, OpenAI Realtime premium' — the $0.00165/min figure is audio-input only; full-duplex with output is meaningfully higher. The '100× cost win' claim needs the actual math shown.
Plan says 'basic-memory read-only sync' but doesn't say how — Obsidian vault sync to iOS without iCloud or a third-party sync service is non-trivial. SAF on Android is also limited for large vaults.
Smart Connections WebGL graph viewer is listed casually but is a real visualization engineering task with performance ceilings on phones (>2000 nodes degrades on mid-tier hardware).

App Intents catalog of 10 intents is listed as a Phase 3 deliverable but each intent requires careful schema design, donation, parameter resolution, and snippet view — realistically ~2-3 days each, so ~3-4 weeks just for App Intents.
No mention of crash reporting, analytics, or error telemetry infrastructure — solo dev needs this to triage production issues without being chained to the device.
No mention of how Fritz tests Wear OS without a Wear device, or watchOS approval flows without a paired Apple Watch — both are required hardware purchases.
WhisperKit 'Large V3 Turbo bundled' adds 1.6GB to IPA — this is on top of the Gemma bundle. The plan double-counts on-device model weight elsewhere.

Plan does not address how Fritz will reconcile his existing FAMtastic 'Studio is managed by launchd, never start manually' rule with a phone-triggered relay that needs to wake/restart the desk runtime — there is a real coordination question with the existing launchd contract.

strengths: All four blockers from the prior critique are now decisively resolved: computer-use bridge cut to Px, bundled LLM weights removed, background voice constrained to CallKit, and destructive App Intents gated on biometric. The plan no longer hand-waves Guideline 2.5.2, 2.5.4, 4.2.7, or 5.1.1.

KMP rejection is now correctly defended against the actual repo state — 153 Swift files in shay-desktop, zero Kotlin files anywhere — and the dual-store, dual-toolchain, dual-on-device-LLM tax is named honestly. iOS-only V1 is the right solo-dev call.

The on-device story is now honest: Tier A (NLTagger + CoreML, every device, ~5MB bundle) is load-bearing and ubiquitous; Tier B (Apple FM) is bonus on eligible devices. This decouples the routing-layer narrative from FM's 4K ceiling and ~30–40% install-base eligibility — both of which are real and were ignored in V1.

MCP is correctly repositioned as proxy-display through a typed JSON envelope on shay-relay rather than a from-scratch KMP client of stdio/SSE/sampling/resources/prompts/roots. Saves weeks of reimplementation while preserving the user-visible value.

Phase plan is now honest: 6+6+8=20 weeks, each phase ends with a TestFlight build and a named exit gate, and the comparison-shopping/BYOK framing keeps the brain-switcher UI inside vendor TOS. Risk #9 was correctly upgraded from Low to Medium.

Launchd contract is now reconciled cleanly — shay-relay is its own job, never starts/restarts com.famtastic.studio. Verified against the existing ~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.famtastic.studio.plist, which matches the plan's described behavior.

Per-provider consent screens (Anthropic / OpenAI / Google) instead of aggregated disclosure clears the 5.1.1 rejection vector that the V1 draft would have hit, and the Apple Sign-In-only stance sidesteps 4.8 parity until Android returns.
minor_issues: Phase 3 App Intents math doesn't add up: §10 says '10 intents; each intent budgeted at 2–3 days … total ~3 weeks.' Ten intents at 2.5 days is 25 days ≈ 5 weeks, not 3. Either trim the catalog to ~6 intents for V1 or extend the App Intents budget — leaving the arithmetic inconsistent will bite at week 16.

Hours-vs-weeks reconciliation is off. §11 says 410 Fritz hours at 25 focused hrs/week, which is ~16.4 weeks, but §10 and §11 both promise 20 weeks. The slack is presumably ramp/research/admin, but the plan should either name that explicitly (e.g. '410 build hours + ~90h ramp/admin = 500h / 25h-wk = 20 weeks') or fix one of the numbers.

Tailscale-based WoL is hand-waved. Tailscale doesn't forward magic packets through the tunnel — WoL requires a Tailscale subnet router on the LAN to relay the broadcast, and Macs are notoriously flaky about WoL over Wi-Fi (and won't respond from deep sleep / Power Nap states without specific firmware support). For Fritz's always-on Mac mini this is fine, but §5's 'phone calls a tiny /wake endpoint on the home router's Tailscale node' implies a topology that needs a Tailscale subnet router with WoL-broadcast permissions configured. Spell that out or drop the WoL path and rely on the always-on assumption.

iOS 26 SpeechAnalyzer is the Phase 2 default STT, but the plan never names a deployment target or a fallback for pre-iOS-26 devices. If V1 ships in late 2026 the eligible base is likely fine, but with no fallback path and WhisperKit explicitly removed, any user on iOS 17/18 has no STT in V1. Either set deployment target to iOS 26 (and own the install-base cost in §2 footnote) or budget a fallback STT path in Phase 2.

Watch approve-from-wrist UX gets degraded by the (correct) biometric round-trip on the paired phone. The plan calls it 'non-negotiable' for compliance but doesn't say the obvious thing to the user: you still have to pick up your phone, so 'approve from wrist' really means 'see the approval prompt on your wrist; finish it on your phone.' Flag this in §8 / §3 #10 so the value prop isn't oversold.

Crash reporting / analytics / telemetry is parked in Phase 3, but TestFlight #1 in Phase 1 will be running for 14 weeks before observability lands. Move at least Sentry (or equivalent) and a minimal event pipeline into Phase 1 so Phase 1+2 TestFlight crashes are debuggable.

'frequent-updates entitlement requested but not assumed; Apple approval can take 1–4 weeks' is slightly outdated. NSSupportsLiveActivitiesFrequentUpdates is a self-declared Info.plist key, not an Apple-granted entitlement — it's enforced by an OS-level energy budget at runtime, not by an approval queue. The mitigation (graceful fallback) is still correct, but the framing in §9, §11, and Risk #7 should be updated to 'system-throttled' rather than 'Apple-approved.'

Cloudflare Tunnel listed as a fallback to the Tailscale onboarding cliff in Risk #3 is a one-liner that masks a real second design (CF Access policy, tunnel daemon on the desk, public hostname). Either remove it as a V1 mitigation or name the additional design cost. As written it makes the cliff sound shallower than it is.

iCloud Drive basic-memory sync in Phase 3 isn't risk-rated. UIDocument file providers over an Obsidian vault with thousands of small markdown files have well-known conflict and indexing issues, especially when the desk is writing concurrently. Add a row to §12 or downgrade the feature to a read-only snapshot pull-on-demand rather than a sync.

§5 mentions 'APNs auth-key rotation' as a relay responsibility. APNs .p8 auth keys are long-lived credentials (Apple revokes only on request); they don't rotate on a schedule. The relay should generate provider tokens (JWTs signed with the .p8) and rotate those every ~30–60 min per APNs guidance — that's the actual operational concern. Minor phrasing fix.

major_issues.claim: Apple Foundation Models 'eligible device' framing still understates the eligibility gate and the App Store risk of advertising FM-dependent features.
major_issues.rebuttal: §7 narrows FM eligibility to 'iPhone 15 Pro / 16+ in 2026, ~30-40% active install' and treats FM as bonus on top of Tier A. That's directionally right, but the plan still ships an Offline mode row in §2 marked '✓ [P1] FM-eligible devices; queued-for-cloud on others' and a brain-switcher row that lists 'On-device FM (eligible only)' as a first-class brain. App Store reviewers and users on non-eligible hardware will see those affordances and have them silently disappear. Apple's HIG (Apple Intelligence section) and the App Review precedent on Apple Intelligence-only features both push toward (a) feature-flagging the FM brain entirely out of the switcher on ineligible devices rather than greying it, and (b) not marketing 'true offline' as a V1 capability when the majority install base only gets the 'queued' fallback. The plan should either drop 'Offline mode (true)' from the V1 wedge or rename the row to 'Offline drafting on Apple Intelligence devices; queued elsewhere' so the matrix is not overstating. Same for the §3 #12 'Offline path' headline.

major_issues.claim: Apple Foundation Models availability cannot be assumed for an app that ships to all iOS 18/26 devices without an explicit Apple Intelligence runtime check and a documented fallback that survives the user toggling Apple Intelligence off.
major_issues.rebuttal: The plan treats FM as 'provisioned by the OS' and 'free on eligible devices.' That's true only when (1) the device is on the eligible SKU list, (2) the user is in a supported region/language, (3) Apple Intelligence is enabled in Settings, and (4) the model assets have finished their on-device download (which can take hours after first opt-in and re-downloads after OS updates). None of those are addressed. Risk #4 covers the 4K ceiling but not the availability matrix. Concretely: a user on an iPhone 15 Pro in an unsupported locale, or with Apple Intelligence toggled off, or mid-asset-download, will get a SystemLanguageModel.availability != .available result and the FM brain row in §3 #1 and the offline path in §3 #12 both silently fail. The plan needs an explicit availability check in the routing decision tree in §7 and a UX state for 'FM unavailable on this device right now' that is distinct from 'FM not on this hardware.' Otherwise the V1 wedge claim 'On-device LLM (any) ✓ [P1]' is fragile against reviewer testing and against the 1.5 / 2.3.1 'accurately represent functionality' guideline.

major_issues.claim: The 'BYOK-only, no IAP' posture in §9 needs a sharper read of Guideline 3.1.1 around external account configuration and reader-app rules.
major_issues.rebuttal: §9 says 'V1 is BYOK-only with no in-app subscription or token-pack purchases. Account creation does not link to a website billing page.' That's the right instinct but the guideline surface is more specific than the plan acknowledges. 3.1.1 forbids buttons, external links, or other calls to action that direct customers to purchasing mechanisms other than IAP for digital content/services used in the app. The brain switcher exposes Anthropic/OpenAI/Google as in-app services consumed via user-supplied keys; if the onboarding has any 'Get an Anthropic key' or 'Sign up at console.anthropic.com' link, that is the exact pattern that has triggered rejections in BYOK AI apps in 2025-2026. The mitigation is to (a) require keys to be pasted from outside the app with no in-app link to the vendor's signup or billing, (b) document this in the App Review notes, and (c) confirm the reader-app/External Link Account entitlement is not needed. The plan should call this out in §6 (auth + pair) and §9, not leave it implicit. Risk #9 in §12 covers comparison-shopping TOS but not the 3.1.1 external-link surface.

minor_issues: §2 capability matrix marks 'On-device LLM (any) ✓ [P1] Apple FM on eligible devices only' — the parenthetical belongs in the legend column too, otherwise the row reads as a flat win versus Claude/ChatGPT which is misleading on non-eligible devices.

§7 'Tier A latency budget ≤30ms p95 on A15+' is reasonable but the plan should specify the floor device the app supports. If V1 supports iOS 17 minimum that includes A13 (iPhone 11), where NLTagger + a custom CoreML classifier will routinely exceed 100ms warm-start. The 'dispatch optimistically to last-selected brain' fallback works, but the supported-device floor should be stated in §10 exit gates.

§9 mentions iOS 26 SpeechAnalyzer as default STT — the deployment target needs to be explicit. If V1 requires iOS 26, the addressable install base in 2026 is materially smaller than the FM eligibility cohort and the plan should say so; if V1 supports iOS 18, there needs to be an SFSpeechRecognizer fallback path documented for Phase 2.

§8 Live Activities '4 simultaneous cap' — iOS itself caps active Live Activities per app and the system may evict at its discretion; the plan should note that the 4 cap is a self-imposed UX limit, not the OS guarantee, and that eviction is observable via ActivityKit state.

§9 'Age rating 17+ to match Claude/ChatGPT' — fine, but if any App Intent surfaces output through Siri/voice-only contexts, App Review has been stricter about unrestricted generative output in those surfaces; worth noting in the App Intents catalog work in P3.

§11 'APNs auth key rotation: budget ~1hr per rotation; need P256 keys' — APNs auth keys (.p8) don't expire and don't need rotation on a schedule; the line conflates auth keys with provider tokens (which are JWTs signed with the .p8 and rotate hourly). Worth correcting so the ops budget reflects the JWT-refresh cron, not a key rotation ritual.
§5 architecture diagram shows 'APNs auth-key rotation' inside shay-relay — same conflation; the relay rotates the JWT, not the auth key.
strengths: Computer-use bridge is cleanly cut to Px and the 4.2.7 reasoning is correctly stated rather than hand-waved.
Bundled LLM weights are removed from V1 with the right reasoning (App Thinning contradiction, install conversion, FM provisioned by OS).
Background voice is correctly scoped to CallKit-only, which is the actual mitigation for the 2.5.4 rejection pattern that has hit ChatGPT-clones.
App Intents destructive composition gating on biometric is the right call and the plan accepts the cost to the voice-composition narrative honestly.
KMP rejection for V1 is well-defended against Fritz's actual skill stack and the 20-week timeline; the Swift-shared-core-now / Kotlin-extract-later sequencing is the right solo-dev move.
Per-provider consent screens replacing aggregated disclosure is the correct read of 5.1.1 / 5.1.2(i) precedent.
Frequent-updates entitlement is requested but not assumed, with graceful fallback documented — the right posture given Apple's inconsistent approval pattern.
Watch-initiated approval round-tripping biometric on the paired phone is the correct mitigation for the 'watch unlocked, phone locked, consequential action' rejection vector.
Launchd contract reconciliation (shay-relay as its own job, never touches com.famtastic.studio) honors the project's non-negotiable studio process rule.
